features.summary: Nike reported Q1 FY2022 revenue growth of 16% with strong digital momentum (25% growth) and EBIT growth of 22%. Digital now represents 21% of Nike brand revenue, up 2 points year-over-year. The company maintained strong brand positioning and full-price realization above 65% target, but faces significant supply chain disruptions from Vietnam factory closures and extended transit times that have doubled to 80 days.
features.management_outlook: Nike revised FY2022 revenue guidance to mid-single digit growth from prior low double-digit expectations due to supply chain constraints. The company expects Q2 revenue to be flat to down low single digits, with inventory shortages continuing for several quarters. Management remains confident in long-term prospects and expects inventory supply to improve heading into FY2023, maintaining investments in digital transformation and brand building despite near-term headwinds.
features.risks: Primary risks include ongoing Vietnam factory closures (80% of footwear factories closed since mid-July), extended transit times creating inventory shortages, and potential COVID-related disruptions in key markets. Greater China faces particular vulnerability due to lower in-transit inventory levels and shorter lead times. The company also faces elevated transportation and logistics costs, with approximately 100 basis points impact on gross margins from freight surcharges.
features.key_drivers: Digital transformation continues driving growth with 40% digital growth in North America and strong member engagement (70% growth in repeat buying members). The Consumer Direct Acceleration strategy is successfully shifting the business model toward higher-margin direct sales. Strong brand momentum from Olympics performance, return of sports, and innovative product launches like Space Hippie sustainability line are maintaining premium positioning and pricing power despite supply constraints.
